meta.title: SF-SPC-1: Architecture, Virtual Warehouses and Storage

questions.stem: Which layer of Snowflake's architecture is responsible for authentication, query optimization, and metadata management?

options.text: Storage layer
options.explanation: The storage layer is responsible for persisting data in columnar micro-partitions on cloud object storage.

options.text: Compute layer
options.explanation: The compute layer (virtual warehouses) executes queries using MPP clusters.

options.text: Cloud Services layer
options.explanation: The Cloud Services layer handles authentication, query optimization, metadata management, and security — it is the 'brain' of Snowflake.

options.text: Virtual warehouse layer
options.explanation: Virtual warehouses are part of the compute layer, not a separate architectural layer.

questions.stem: What is the compressed size range of a Snowflake micro-partition?

options.explanation: Snowflake micro-partitions range from 50 MB to 500 MB of compressed data.
1–10 MB is too small for a micro-partition.

options.text: 1–5 GB
options.explanation: 1–5 GB would be too large for a micro-partition.
10–100 MB does not match the documented range.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator runs the same SELECT query twice within 10 minutes, with no data changes to the underlying table between executions. Which statement BEST describes what happens during the second execution?

options.text: The query re-executes on the virtual warehouse but reads from SSD cache instead of object storage.
options.explanation: The SSD (local disk) cache requires the virtual warehouse to be active and would still consume credits.

options.text: The query re-executes and consumes compute credits but benefits from metadata pruning.
options.explanation: The query does not re-execute when the result cache is hit.

options.text: The virtual warehouse automatically scales up to return the cached result faster.
options.explanation: Scaling the warehouse is not triggered by cached results.

options.text: The query result is returned from the query result cache in the Cloud Services layer and consumes no compute credits.
options.explanation: The query result cache is held in the Cloud Services layer for 24 hours. If the same query is run by the same role, against unchanged data, the cached result is returned without engaging the virtual warehouse, consuming zero compute credits.

questions.stem: A data engineer resizes a virtual warehouse from X-Small to Small. How many credits per hour will the Small warehouse consume?

options.text: 2 credits per hour
options.explanation: Each warehouse size doubles the credit consumption. X-Small consumes 1 credit/hr, Small consumes 2 credits/hr, Medium consumes 4 credits/hr, and so on.

options.text: 1 credit per hour
options.explanation: 1 credit/hr is the X-Small rate, not the Small rate.

options.text: 4 credits per hour
options.explanation: 4 credits/hr is the Medium rate.

options.text: 8 credits per hour
options.explanation: 8 credits/hr is the Large rate.

questions.stem: Which Snowflake feature allows a single virtual warehouse to automatically add server clusters to handle concurrent user load spikes, then remove them when demand drops?

options.text: Auto-suspend and auto-resume
options.explanation: Auto-suspend/resume controls whether an idle warehouse shuts down, not whether additional clusters are added.

options.text: Query result cache
options.explanation: The query result cache reduces load but does not add compute capacity.

options.text: Search Optimization Service
options.explanation: Search Optimization Service improves selective query performance through a pre-built data structure, not dynamic scaling.

options.text: Multi-cluster warehouses
options.explanation: Multi-cluster warehouses allow Snowflake to automatically provision additional clusters (up to the configured maximum) when query queuing is detected, and remove clusters when demand decreases.

questions.stem: A company processes sensitive healthcare data and requires HIPAA compliance. They also need Time Travel retention of up to 90 days. What is the MINIMUM Snowflake edition that satisfies BOTH requirements?

options.text: Standard
options.explanation: Standard supports only up to 1 day of Time Travel and does not offer HIPAA compliance.

options.text: Enterprise
options.explanation: Enterprise supports up to 90 days of Time Travel but does NOT provide HIPAA/BAA support.

options.text: Business Critical
options.explanation: Business Critical is the minimum edition that provides HIPAA compliance support (through a Business Associate Agreement) AND supports up to 90 days of Time Travel.

options.text: Virtual Private Snowflake
options.explanation: Virtual Private Snowflake does satisfy both requirements, but it is not the MINIMUM — Business Critical already satisfies them at a lower tier.

questions.stem: Which TWO of the following metadata values does Snowflake store for each column within a micro-partition? (Select TWO)

options.text: Min and max values
options.explanation: and **C** are correct because Snowflake stores min/max values and null counts per column within each micro-partition. This metadata is used by the pruning engine to skip micro-partitions that cannot contain matching rows.

options.text: Row-level checksums
options.explanation: Row-level checksums are not stored as per-column metadata in micro-partitions.

options.text: Null value count
Column encryption keys
options.explanation: Encryption keys are managed at the account/key hierarchy level, not stored inside micro-partition column metadata.

questions.stem: A multi-cluster warehouse is configured with MIN_CLUSTER_COUNT = 1 and MAX_CLUSTER_COUNT = 4 using the Standard scaling policy. Under the Standard policy, when does Snowflake add an additional cluster?

options.text: When at least one query is queued for 2 minutes or more.
options.explanation: The 2-minute wait applies to the Economy scaling policy, which is more conservative.

options.text: When CPU utilization on the active cluster exceeds 80% for 5 consecutive minutes.
options.explanation: CPU utilization is not the trigger for multi-cluster scaling in either policy.

options.text: When a queued query is detected, Snowflake immediately provisions an additional cluster.
options.explanation: Under the Standard scaling policy, Snowflake adds a cluster immediately when a query is detected in the queue — the goal is to minimize queuing latency.

options.text: When the number of active queries exceeds a user-defined threshold.
options.explanation: There is no user-defined query threshold for multi-cluster scaling.

questions.stem: Which Snowflake system-defined role is responsible for managing users and roles across the account?

options.text: SYSADMIN
options.explanation: SYSADMIN is responsible for creating and managing database objects (warehouses, databases, schemas, tables).

options.text: SECURITYADMIN
options.explanation: SECURITYADMIN has the privilege to manage all users and roles, including creating and granting roles.

options.text: USERADMIN
options.explanation: USERADMIN can create users and roles but does not have the full account-level privilege to manage all roles as SECURITYADMIN does.

options.text: PUBLIC
options.explanation: PUBLIC is the default role automatically granted to all users and has minimal privileges.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator needs to prevent users from connecting to Snowflake from IP addresses outside of the corporate network. Which object should be used?

options.text: Resource monitor
options.explanation: Resource monitors track and limit credit consumption, not network access.

options.text: Row access policy
options.explanation: Row access policies control which rows a user can see in a table, not network connectivity.

options.text: Network policy
options.explanation: A network policy allows administrators to define allowed and blocked IP address ranges, restricting Snowflake access to specific IP addresses. It can be applied at the account or user level.

options.text: Masking policy
options.explanation: Masking policies control how column data is displayed, not network access.

questions.stem: A company wants to hide the Social Security Number column from all users except those with the 'PII_ADMIN' role. Which Snowflake feature should they use?

options.text: Dynamic Data Masking
options.explanation: Dynamic Data Masking allows a masking policy to be applied to a column that conditionally shows full or masked data based on the current role.

options.text: Row access policy
options.explanation: Row access policies control which rows are returned, not how column values are displayed.

options.text: Secure view
options.explanation: Secure views hide the view definition but do not dynamically mask individual column values based on role.

options.text: Column-level encryption
options.explanation: Column-level encryption is not a native Snowflake object type — Snowflake uses masking policies for this use case.

questions.stem: A developer runs the following SQL: `GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA mydb.myschema TO ROLE analyst;`. A new table is created in mydb.myschema the next day. Which statement is TRUE about the analyst role's access to the new table?

options.text: The analyst role automatically has SELECT on the new table because the grant was on ALL TABLES.
options.explanation: The grant is not retroactively applied to future objects.

options.text: The analyst role inherits SELECT on the new table if it was granted the USAGE privilege on the schema.
options.explanation: USAGE on a schema allows the schema to be accessed but does not grant SELECT on individual tables.

options.text: The analyst role has SELECT on the new table only if the table was created by SYSADMIN.
options.explanation: The table creator's role does not affect whether analyst inherits privileges.

options.text: The analyst role does NOT have SELECT on the new table; the grant applied only to tables that existed at grant time.
options.explanation: `GRANT ... ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA` is a one-time grant that applies only to tables existing at the time the grant is executed. Future tables require a separate `GRANT` or the use of `GRANT ... ON FUTURE TABLES IN SCHEMA` to automatically include new tables.

questions.stem: Which TWO statements correctly describe Snowflake's role hierarchy and privilege inheritance? (Select TWO)

options.text: Privileges granted to a child role are inherited by any parent role that the child role is granted to.
options.explanation: and **C** are correct because privilege inheritance flows upward through the role hierarchy — a parent role inherits all privileges held by child roles granted to it. The syntax `GRANT ROLE child TO ROLE parent` is the correct way to establish this hierarchy.

options.text: ACCOUNTADMIN is the only role that can grant privileges on account-level objects.
options.explanation: SECURITYADMIN and SYSADMIN can also manage various account-level privileges within their scope.

options.text: A role can be granted to another role using GRANT ROLE child_role TO ROLE parent_role.
Users can only belong to one role at a time; they must switch roles to use different privileges.
options.explanation: Users can have multiple roles granted to them, though they can only have one 'active' role per session; however, the hierarchy means parent roles accumulate privileges from child roles regardless.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator wants to enforce Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) for all users in the account. Which MFA provider does Snowflake natively integrate with?

options.text: Google Authenticator
options.explanation: Google Authenticator is not the native MFA provider used by Snowflake's built-in MFA feature.

options.text: Microsoft Authenticator
options.explanation: Microsoft Authenticator is not the native MFA provider.

options.text: Duo Security
options.explanation: Snowflake's built-in MFA integration uses Duo Security for push notifications and passcodes.

options.text: Okta Verify
options.explanation: Okta Verify is an SSO/SAML provider integration, not the native Snowflake MFA solution.

questions.stem: A data engineer uses the Query Profile in Snowsight to investigate a slow query. Which view does the Query Profile display?

options.text: A list of all tables scanned in alphabetical order
options.explanation: The Query Profile does not display a simple alphabetical table list.

options.text: An operator tree showing execution nodes with statistics like rows in/out and partition pruning
options.explanation: The Query Profile displays an interactive operator tree (DAG) where each node shows statistics such as rows processed, bytes processed, and how many partitions were scanned vs. pruned. This helps identify bottlenecks.

options.text: A timeline of SQL statements executed in the session
options.explanation: A session history list is available elsewhere in Snowsight, not in the Query Profile.

options.text: The execution plan in text format identical to EXPLAIN output
options.explanation: The EXPLAIN command outputs a text-based plan, while the Query Profile is an interactive visual tool with live execution statistics.

questions.stem: A table has been loaded with rows in random order for its `ORDER_DATE` column. Analysts frequently filter on `ORDER_DATE`. After running `ALTER TABLE orders CLUSTER BY (order_date)`, what happens to the existing data?

options.text: The table is immediately and fully reorganized in the background with no credit cost.
options.explanation: Reclustering is not free — it consumes compute credits.

options.text: The clustering key only affects newly inserted rows; existing micro-partitions are never reorganized.
options.explanation: Reclustering does reorganize existing data over time, not only new inserts.

options.text: The CLUSTER BY statement locks the table until all existing data is reorganized.
options.explanation: The CLUSTER BY statement does not lock the table.

options.text: Snowflake will periodically recluster the table using compute credits, reorganizing micro-partitions to improve ORDER_DATE locality.
options.explanation: When you define a clustering key, Snowflake's automatic reclustering service periodically reorganizes micro-partitions in the background using compute credits to improve clustering depth for the specified key. This is an ongoing process, not an immediate one-time operation.

questions.stem: Which cache type in Snowflake is stored on the SSD of virtual warehouse nodes and is lost when the warehouse is suspended?

options.text: Query result cache
options.explanation: The query result cache is stored in the Cloud Services layer and persists for 24 hours regardless of warehouse state.

options.text: Metadata cache
options.explanation: The metadata cache is also in the Cloud Services layer and persists independently of warehouse state.

options.text: Local disk cache
options.explanation: The local disk cache (also called the data cache or warehouse cache) stores recently accessed micro-partition data on the SSD of warehouse nodes. It is ephemeral — when the warehouse suspends, the cache is lost.

options.text: Remote storage cache
options.explanation: 'remote storage cache' is not a Snowflake cache type.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator checks `SYSTEM$CLUSTERING_INFORMATION('orders', '(order_date)')` and receives an `average_depth` of 8. What does this indicate?

options.text: The table has 8 micro-partitions, which is optimal.
options.explanation: Average_depth refers to overlap depth, not the count of micro-partitions.

options.text: On average, 8 micro-partitions overlap for any given ORDER_DATE value, indicating poor clustering.
options.explanation: `average_depth` represents the average number of micro-partitions that overlap for any given value of the clustering key column(s). A lower depth means better clustering — a depth of 8 is high, meaning data for a given ORDER_DATE is spread across many micro-partitions and pruning will be less effective. A target depth is generally below 3 for actively queried tables.

options.text: The clustering key spans 8 columns, which exceeds the recommended maximum.
options.explanation: Average_depth does not refer to the number of columns in the clustering key.

options.text: 8% of micro-partitions are fully pruned by the clustering key, which is suboptimal.
options.explanation: Average_depth is not a percentage metric.

questions.stem: Which Snowflake feature is MOST appropriate for dramatically improving the performance of point-lookup queries (equality filters on highly selective columns) on very large tables, without requiring manual partition management?

options.text: Materialized views
options.explanation: Materialized views are beneficial for pre-aggregating complex queries but are not specifically designed for high-cardinality point lookups.

options.text: Clustering keys
options.explanation: Clustering keys help with range scans and date-based filtering but are less effective for random equality lookups on high-cardinality columns.

options.text: Search Optimization Service
options.explanation: The Search Optimization Service builds a persistent search access path that dramatically improves the performance of equality and range predicate queries on selective high-cardinality columns — exactly the point-lookup use case.

options.text: Multi-cluster warehouses
options.explanation: Multi-cluster warehouses address concurrency, not individual query performance.

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to load data from files stored in an Amazon S3 bucket into a Snowflake table. Which Snowflake object is used to define the connection to the external S3 bucket?

options.text: External stage
options.explanation: An external stage is the Snowflake object that defines a reference to an external location (S3, Azure Blob, or GCS) including the URL and credentials (or storage integration). The COPY INTO command then uses the external stage to load data.

options.text: Internal stage
options.explanation: An internal stage stores files within Snowflake's own managed storage, not external cloud storage.

options.text: Pipe
options.explanation: A Pipe (Snowpipe) is used for continuous data loading and references an existing stage — it does not define the external connection.

options.text: Storage integration
options.explanation: A storage integration is a separate Snowflake object used for authentication to cloud storage, but it is not the stage itself.

questions.stem: A data engineer runs a COPY INTO command and sets ON_ERROR = 'SKIP_FILE'. A data file contains 5 rows and 2 of them have parse errors. What happens?

options.text: The 3 valid rows are loaded; the 2 error rows are skipped.
options.explanation: SKIP_FILE skips the entire file, not just the individual error rows. CONTINUE is the option that skips individual error rows and loads the rest.

options.text: The load aborts immediately and no files are processed.
options.explanation: ABORT_STATEMENT is the option that aborts the entire COPY INTO operation immediately.

options.text: The entire file is skipped; no rows from that file are loaded.
options.explanation: ON_ERROR = 'SKIP_FILE' causes Snowflake to skip the entire file if any error is encountered within it — none of the rows in that file are loaded.

options.text: The 2 error rows are logged and the remaining 3 rows are loaded after 5 minutes.
options.explanation: There is no delayed retry mechanism in COPY INTO.

questions.stem: A team wants to continuously ingest JSON files from an Azure Blob Storage container into Snowflake with minimal latency. They need the ingestion to trigger automatically when new files arrive. Which configuration is MOST appropriate?

options.text: A Snowpipe with AUTO_INGEST = TRUE configured with Azure Event Grid notifications.
options.explanation: Snowpipe with AUTO_INGEST = TRUE on Azure uses Azure Event Grid notifications to automatically trigger file ingestion as soon as new files land in the Azure Blob container. This provides near-real-time, event-driven ingestion.

options.text: A scheduled task running COPY INTO every minute.
options.explanation: A scheduled task with COPY INTO introduces unnecessary latency (the full interval between runs) and is not truly event-driven.

options.text: A Snowpipe called via the REST API on a fixed schedule.
options.explanation: The Snowpipe REST API requires external orchestration to call it, which defeats the purpose of automatic event-driven ingestion.

options.text: An external stage with FORCE = TRUE to reload files continuously.
options.explanation: FORCE = TRUE in COPY INTO simply forces reloading of already-loaded files and does not enable event-driven triggering.

questions.stem: What is the purpose of the `METADATA$ACTION` column in a Snowflake stream?

options.text: It indicates whether the row represents an INSERT or DELETE operation.
options.explanation: METADATA$ACTION contains either 'INSERT' or 'DELETE' to indicate the type of DML change captured in the stream. Updates appear as paired DELETE + INSERT rows.

options.text: It stores the user who made the change.
options.explanation: The stream does not capture the user who made the change; that information is in the query history.

options.text: It contains a unique row identifier that never changes.
options.explanation: METADATA$ROW_ID is the column that provides a unique row identifier.

options.text: It stores the timestamp when the change was captured.
options.explanation: The stream does not expose a dedicated timestamp column for the change time.

questions.stem: A data engineer creates a stream on a table that only receives INSERT operations (no UPDATEs or DELETEs). Which stream type provides the MOST efficient processing for this use case?

options.text: Standard stream
options.explanation: A standard stream tracks all DML (inserts, updates, deletes), which adds overhead not needed for insert-only workloads.

options.text: Insert-only stream
options.explanation: Insert-only streams are specifically for external tables, not standard Snowflake tables.

options.text: Delta stream
options.explanation: 'delta stream' is not a Snowflake stream type.

options.text: Append-only stream
options.explanation: An append-only stream is optimized for tables that receive only insert operations. It tracks inserted rows without the overhead of tracking update/delete pairs, making it more efficient for insert-heavy workloads.

questions.stem: A task is created with the following DDL: `CREATE TASK load_task WAREHOUSE = 'TRANSFORM_WH' SCHEDULE = 'USING CRON 0 2 * * * America/New_York' AS INSERT INTO ...`. What must be done before this task will run?

options.text: The task must be resumed with ALTER TASK load_task RESUME.
options.explanation: Tasks are created in a suspended state by default. They must be explicitly resumed using `ALTER TASK task_name RESUME` before they will execute on schedule.

options.text: Nothing; tasks start running immediately after creation.
options.explanation: Tasks do NOT start immediately — they begin in a suspended state.

options.text: The warehouse must be manually started before the scheduled time.
options.explanation: Snowflake automatically resumes the virtual warehouse when a task is scheduled to run (assuming the warehouse has auto-resume enabled).

options.text: The task must be tested with EXECUTE TASK load_task before it can run on schedule.
options.explanation: EXECUTE TASK is an optional manual trigger for testing; it is not required before scheduled runs.

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to implement a CDC pipeline using Snowflake streams and tasks. Which TWO statements about streams are CORRECT? (Select TWO)

options.text: A stream becomes stale if its offset advances beyond the Time Travel retention period of the source table.
options.explanation: and **B** are correct. A stream becomes stale if its offset falls behind beyond the Time Travel retention period — after that, the captured change data is no longer available. When a stream is consumed inside a DML transaction (e.g., INSERT INTO ... SELECT FROM stream), the offset advances only if the transaction commits; if it rolls back, the stream offset remains unchanged.

options.text: Consuming a stream in a DML transaction advances the stream offset only if the transaction commits successfully.
Multiple streams on the same table share the same offset position.
options.explanation: Each stream on a table maintains its own independent offset.

options.text: SYSTEM$STREAM_HAS_DATA() returns TRUE even if no new rows have been inserted since the stream was last consumed.
options.explanation: SYSTEM$STREAM_HAS_DATA() returns FALSE when there is no new unconsumed data in the stream.

questions.stem: A developer creates a Dynamic Table with `TARGET_LAG = '5 minutes'`. Which statement BEST describes this setting?

options.text: Snowflake will refresh the dynamic table exactly every 5 minutes using a fixed cron schedule.
options.explanation: TARGET_LAG is not a fixed cron schedule — it is a staleness target that drives Snowflake's automated refresh scheduling.

options.text: Snowflake guarantees the dynamic table data will never be more than 5 minutes behind its base tables.
options.explanation: The guarantee framing is too strong; it is a target, not a hard SLA.

options.text: Snowflake will attempt to refresh the dynamic table so that it lags its base tables by no more than 5 minutes, on a best-effort basis.
options.explanation: TARGET_LAG specifies the target (not guaranteed) maximum staleness of the dynamic table relative to its base tables. Snowflake schedules refreshes to meet this target on a best-effort basis.

options.text: The dynamic table will only refresh when data older than 5 minutes is detected in the base tables.
options.explanation: The refresh is not triggered by data age within the base tables beyond the lag window.

questions.stem: Which stored procedure execution mode allows the procedure to operate with the privileges of the role that calls the procedure, rather than the role that owns it?

options.text: EXECUTE AS OWNER
options.explanation: EXECUTE AS OWNER (the default) causes the procedure to run with the privileges of the procedure owner's role, regardless of who calls it.

options.text: EXECUTE AS CALLER
options.explanation: EXECUTE AS CALLER causes the stored procedure to run with the privileges of the calling role, allowing the procedure's access to be scoped to what the caller can do.

options.text: EXECUTE AS CURRENT_USER
options.explanation: EXECUTE AS CURRENT_USER is not a valid Snowflake stored procedure execution option.

options.text: EXECUTE AS SECURITYADMIN
options.explanation: EXECUTE AS SECURITYADMIN is not a valid option.

questions.stem: How long is Snowflake's Fail-Safe period for standard tables?

options.text: 1 day
options.explanation: 1 day is the default Time Travel retention for Standard edition, not the Fail-Safe duration.

options.text: 14 days
options.explanation: 14 days is not the Fail-Safe duration.

options.text: 30 days
options.explanation: 30 days is not the Fail-Safe duration.

options.text: 7 days
options.explanation: Snowflake Fail-Safe provides a 7-day period after the Time Travel retention period expires during which Snowflake can recover data for disaster recovery purposes.

questions.stem: A data engineer accidentally drops a table in Snowflake. The table had a Time Travel retention period of 24 hours and was dropped 3 hours ago. Which command restores the table?

options.text: UNDROP TABLE orders;
options.explanation: `UNDROP TABLE` restores a dropped table from Time Travel. Since the table was dropped only 3 hours ago and the retention period is 24 hours, the table is still within Time Travel and can be restored.

options.text: RESTORE TABLE orders;
options.explanation: RESTORE TABLE is not a valid Snowflake command.

options.text: RECOVER TABLE orders;
options.explanation: RECOVER TABLE is not a valid Snowflake command.

options.text: SELECT * FROM orders AT(OFFSET => -10800);
options.explanation: AT(OFFSET => ...) queries historical data but does not restore a dropped table — it would fail since the table no longer exists.

questions.stem: What is the default auto-suspend timeout for a newly created Snowflake virtual warehouse?

options.text: 1 minute
options.explanation: 1 minute is not the default.

options.text: 5 minutes
options.explanation: 5 minutes is not the default, though it is a commonly used setting.

options.text: 10 minutes
options.explanation: The default AUTO_SUSPEND value for a new virtual warehouse is 600 seconds (10 minutes) of inactivity.

options.text: 30 minutes
options.explanation: 30 minutes would result in excessive credit waste for idle warehouses.

questions.stem: A virtual warehouse is suspended. A user submits a query that requires it. If AUTO_RESUME is set to TRUE, what happens?

options.text: The query fails with an error and the user must manually resume the warehouse.
options.explanation: AUTO_RESUME = TRUE means the user does not need to manually resume the warehouse.

options.text: The query is routed to a different warehouse automatically.
options.explanation: Snowflake does not automatically reroute queries to a different warehouse.

options.text: Snowflake provisions a serverless compute pool instead of resuming the warehouse.
options.explanation: Serverless compute is not provisioned in place of a named virtual warehouse.

options.text: The warehouse resumes automatically and the query is queued until the warehouse is ready.
options.explanation: When AUTO_RESUME = TRUE, Snowflake automatically resumes a suspended warehouse when a query is submitted to it. The query waits in the queue during the brief startup period (typically a few seconds).

questions.stem: A company runs a reporting workload with 200 concurrent users that frequently causes query queuing. They want to scale to handle the concurrency without increasing query execution time per query. Which approach is BEST?

options.text: Resize the warehouse from Large to 4X-Large.
options.explanation: Resizing a warehouse to 4X-Large makes each individual query faster (more resources per query) but does not increase the number of concurrent queries that can run simultaneously.

options.text: Enable multi-cluster warehouse with MIN_CLUSTER_COUNT = 1 and MAX_CLUSTER_COUNT = 4.
options.explanation: Multi-cluster warehouses address concurrency by adding additional clusters when queuing is detected, distributing concurrent queries across clusters. This is the purpose-built Snowflake feature for high-concurrency workloads.

options.text: Enable the Query Acceleration Service on the warehouse.
options.explanation: The Query Acceleration Service offloads portions of long-running queries to serverless compute but does not address concurrent user queuing.

options.text: Increase the AUTO_SUSPEND value to prevent the warehouse from suspending between queries.
options.explanation: Increasing AUTO_SUSPEND does not help with concurrency — it just wastes credits by keeping an idle warehouse running longer.

questions.stem: How does Snowflake bill for virtual warehouse compute usage?

options.text: Per query executed, based on the number of rows processed.
options.explanation: Billing is time-based, not per-query or per-row.

options.text: Per hour, rounded up to the nearest full hour, regardless of actual run time.
options.explanation: Snowflake does not round up to the nearest full hour — per-second billing (after the 60-second minimum) applies.

options.text: Per gigabyte of data scanned during query execution.
options.explanation: Data scanned determines storage and query costs in some models, but virtual warehouse compute is billed by time, not data scanned.

options.text: Per second the warehouse is running, with a minimum 60-second charge per start.
options.explanation: Snowflake bills for virtual warehouses per second of runtime, but applies a minimum charge of 60 seconds each time a warehouse starts (resumes). After the initial 60 seconds, billing is per-second.

questions.stem: A data engineering team runs heavy ETL jobs on an X-Large warehouse. They want to reduce credit costs without affecting job completion time for queries that use large table scans. Which Snowflake feature allows certain parts of a query to be offloaded to auto-provisioned serverless resources, reducing the warehouse size needed?

options.text: Concurrency scaling
options.explanation: Concurrency scaling adds warehouse clusters to handle more simultaneous queries but does not reduce the resources used per query.

options.text: Automatic clustering
options.explanation: Automatic clustering reorganizes data but does not dynamically offload query execution.

options.text: Query Acceleration Service
options.explanation: The Query Acceleration Service (QAS) offloads portions of eligible queries (particularly large table scans) to serverless compute resources. This can allow a smaller warehouse to handle queries that previously required a larger one, reducing credit costs.

options.text: Search Optimization Service
options.explanation: The Search Optimization Service speeds up selective point-lookup queries but does not offload compute to serverless resources.

questions.stem: A multi-cluster warehouse is running with MAX_CLUSTER_COUNT = 3 and the Economy scaling policy. Which TWO statements correctly describe the Economy scaling policy behavior? (Select TWO)

options.text: The Economy policy adds a new cluster only after queued queries have been waiting for approximately 5–6 minutes.
options.explanation: and **C** are correct. The Economy scaling policy is more conservative than Standard — it waits approximately 5–6 minutes of queuing before adding an additional cluster, accepting more queue time to save credits. This is in contrast to the Standard policy which adds a cluster immediately when queuing is detected.

options.text: The Economy policy removes a cluster as soon as all its active queries complete.
options.explanation: The Economy policy does not remove clusters immediately when queries finish; it waits until a cluster has been idle for several consecutive minutes to avoid rapid scale-up/scale-down cycling.

options.text: The Economy policy conserves credits by tolerating more query queuing than the Standard policy.
The Economy policy always keeps all clusters running at the configured MAX_CLUSTER_COUNT.
options.explanation: Both policies scale down when demand drops — neither policy keeps all clusters permanently running at MAX_CLUSTER_COUNT.

questions.stem: A company wants to use a resource monitor to alert when a virtual warehouse has consumed 80% of its monthly credit quota and automatically suspend the warehouse at 100%. At which level must the resource monitor be created to control a specific warehouse?

options.text: Account level, using CREATE RESOURCE MONITOR with CREDIT_QUOTA.
options.explanation: is partially correct that the monitor is created with `CREATE RESOURCE MONITOR`, but that statement alone does not assign it to a specific warehouse.

options.text: Database level, applying the monitor to all objects within a database.
options.explanation: Resource monitors cannot be applied at the database level.

options.text: Resource monitors can only be applied at the account level, not individual warehouses.
options.explanation: Resource monitors can indeed be assigned to individual warehouses.

options.text: Warehouse level, by assigning the monitor to the warehouse using ALTER WAREHOUSE ... SET RESOURCE_MONITOR.
options.explanation: A resource monitor is created at the account level (by ACCOUNTADMIN) but is then assigned to a specific warehouse using `ALTER WAREHOUSE warehouse_name SET RESOURCE_MONITOR = monitor_name`. The monitor tracks credit usage for that specific warehouse.

questions.stem: A virtual warehouse is resized from Medium to X-Large while queries are currently running on it. What is the effect on the running queries?

options.text: Running queries are immediately cancelled and restarted on the larger warehouse.
options.explanation: Snowflake does not cancel running queries during a resize.

options.text: Running queries complete on the current Medium configuration; new queries use the X-Large configuration.
options.explanation: When a warehouse is resized, the change is applied to new query executions. Queries that are currently running continue to completion on the existing server cluster configuration. The new X-Large cluster resources become available for subsequent queries.

options.text: The resize takes effect immediately and running queries automatically benefit from the additional resources.
options.explanation: Running queries cannot dynamically absorb new servers mid-execution.

options.text: The warehouse must be suspended before resizing; the command will fail if queries are running.
options.explanation: The warehouse does not need to be suspended to be resized — resizing is a non-disruptive operation.

questions.stem: What is the purpose of Zero-Copy Cloning in Snowflake?

options.text: It creates a logical copy of an object (table, schema, or database) that initially shares the same underlying micro-partitions as the source, without duplicating storage.
options.explanation: Zero-Copy Cloning creates an independent clone of a table, schema, or database that initially references the same underlying micro-partitions as the source. No data is physically copied at clone time. New writes to the clone (or source) cause copy-on-write — only modified micro-partitions diverge. Storage is only charged for data that changes after cloning.

options.text: It creates a physical copy of all data in a new storage location for backup purposes.
options.explanation: Zero-copy cloning does NOT make a full physical copy — that would defeat the purpose.

options.text: It allows two accounts to share the same table without copying data across accounts.
options.explanation: Cross-account data sharing uses Snowflake's Data Sharing feature (secure views / listings), not zero-copy cloning.

options.text: It compresses data by removing duplicate micro-partitions across all tables in a database.
options.explanation: Zero-copy cloning is not a deduplication or compression mechanism.

questions.stem: A developer queries historical data using: `SELECT * FROM orders AT(TIMESTAMP => '2024-01-15 10:00:00'::TIMESTAMP_TZ);` The table has a 3-day Time Travel retention period. The timestamp in the query is 5 days in the past. What result does the developer receive?

options.text: The data as it existed at that timestamp, retrieved from Fail-Safe storage.
options.explanation: Fail-Safe data is NOT accessible through SQL queries like AT() — Fail-Safe is a Snowflake-managed disaster recovery mechanism, not user-accessible.

options.text: An error indicating the specified timestamp is outside the Time Travel retention period.
options.explanation: The AT clause uses Time Travel, which only retains historical data up to the table's DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS setting. A timestamp 5 days in the past exceeds the 3-day retention period, so Snowflake returns an error stating the timestamp is outside the Time Travel window.

options.text: The current table data, ignoring the historical timestamp.
options.explanation: Snowflake does not silently ignore an invalid AT timestamp.

options.text: An empty result set because data older than 3 days is automatically deleted.
options.explanation: The data is not deleted after 3 days — it enters Fail-Safe — but it is no longer accessible via Time Travel SQL.

questions.stem: A table is created as a TRANSIENT table in Snowflake. Which statement accurately describes the data protection characteristics of a transient table?

options.text: Transient tables have Time Travel up to 90 days and the full 7-day Fail-Safe period.
options.explanation: The 90-day Time Travel maximum applies to permanent tables on Enterprise edition or above.

options.text: Transient tables have Time Travel of 0 or 1 day and NO Fail-Safe period.
options.explanation: Transient tables support Time Travel with a maximum DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS of 1 day (and can be set to 0 to disable), but they have NO Fail-Safe period. This makes transient tables cheaper in terms of storage but provides less data protection. They are appropriate for intermediate/staging data that does not require disaster recovery.

options.text: Transient tables have no Time Travel but retain the 7-day Fail-Safe period.
options.explanation: Transient tables explicitly have no Fail-Safe period.

options.text: Transient tables are identical to permanent tables in data protection but cost less to query.
options.explanation: Transient tables differ significantly from permanent tables in data protection.

questions.stem: How does Snowflake handle data compression for tables stored in its cloud storage layer?

options.text: Snowflake requires users to manually compress data files before loading them; it stores data as-is.
options.explanation: Manual compression is not required; Snowflake handles this automatically.

options.text: Snowflake automatically compresses data using algorithms it selects per column based on data type, and users cannot override this.
options.explanation: is partially correct that compression is automatic, but it overstates the restriction — while users cannot configure per-column compression algorithms directly, the spirit of the question is about automatic compression. C best describes the behavior.

options.text: Snowflake automatically compresses data in micro-partitions, typically using ZSTD or similar algorithms, without requiring user configuration.
options.explanation: Snowflake automatically compresses all data stored in micro-partitions using columnar compression algorithms (such as ZSTD, LZO, or others selected automatically by Snowflake). Users do not need to configure or manage compression — it is fully transparent.

options.text: Data is stored uncompressed in object storage; compression only occurs in transit.
options.explanation: Data is stored in compressed form in object storage, not only during transit.

questions.stem: A data engineer runs `CREATE TABLE orders_clone CLONE orders;`. Which TWO statements about the clone are CORRECT? (Select TWO)

options.text: orders_clone immediately consumes additional storage equal to the full size of the orders table.
options.explanation: Zero-copy cloning does not duplicate storage at clone time — micro-partitions are shared and only diverge (costing new storage) when data is modified in either the source or clone.

options.text: orders_clone is a fully independent table that can be queried, modified, and dropped without affecting orders.
options.explanation: and **C** are correct. The clone is a fully independent Snowflake object — DDL changes, DML, or DROP on the clone do not affect the source table, and vice versa. The clone captures the data state at the time of cloning, including access to the historical micro-partitions up to that point (which enables Time Travel queries on the clone relative to that snapshot).

options.text: The clone includes all data that existed in orders at the time of cloning, including historical Time Travel data.
Grants (privileges) on the source table are automatically inherited by the clone.
options.explanation: Grants are NOT inherited by the clone; you must explicitly grant privileges on the new clone object.

questions.stem: A table has DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 7. The table is dropped. Two days after it is dropped, an administrator decides to restore it using UNDROP TABLE. How much Time Travel history is available on the restored table immediately after UNDROP?

options.text: 7 days of history from before the drop (the full retention period).
options.explanation: The clock does not reset on UNDROP — time elapsed since the drop counts against the retention period.

options.text: 5 days of history, because 2 days of the retention period elapsed while the table was dropped.
options.explanation: When a table is dropped, Time Travel continues to count down against the retention period. The dropped table's retention period began at the time of the drop. Since 2 days have passed since the drop, only 5 days of Time Travel history remain (7 - 2 = 5 days).

options.text: No history; UNDROP restores the table structure only, not historical data.
options.explanation: UNDROP fully restores the table including its historical data; it is not a structure-only operation.

options.text: 7 days of history plus an additional 2 days because Fail-Safe extends the window while a table is dropped.
options.explanation: Fail-Safe begins only after the Time Travel window expires and is not user-accessible through SQL.

questions.stem: Which Snowflake command is used to upload a local file from a client machine to a Snowflake internal stage?

options.text: COPY INTO
options.explanation: COPY INTO loads data from a stage into a table (or unloads from a table to a stage) — it does not upload files from a local machine.

options.text: LOAD DATA
options.explanation: LOAD DATA is not a Snowflake command.

options.text: PUT
options.explanation: The PUT command uploads files from a local file system to a Snowflake internal stage (user stage, table stage, or named internal stage). It must be executed from the SnowSQL CLI or a supported driver/connector; it cannot be run from the Snowsight web UI.

options.text: UPLOAD
options.explanation: UPLOAD is not a valid Snowflake SQL command.

questions.stem: What is the purpose of a Snowflake file format object?

options.text: It defines the physical storage format of micro-partitions on cloud storage.
options.explanation: Micro-partition storage format is internal to Snowflake and not controlled by file format objects.

options.text: It specifies parsing options (delimiter, date format, null handling, etc.) for files used in COPY INTO, PUT, and GET operations.
options.explanation: A named file format object stores a reusable set of options for parsing and formatting data files. This includes settings like the field delimiter, record delimiter, date/time formats, how to handle NULL values, whether the file has a header row, and compression type. These options are referenced by COPY INTO and stage definitions.

options.text: It encrypts files before they are uploaded to an internal stage.
options.explanation: File format objects do not handle encryption — Snowflake encrypts staged files automatically.

options.text: It schedules file compression tasks to run during off-peak hours.
options.explanation: File format objects have no scheduling functionality.

questions.stem: A data engineer runs `COPY INTO my_table FROM @my_stage/data.csv.gz;`. The file was already loaded successfully 2 days ago. What happens by default?

options.text: The file is loaded again, creating duplicate rows.
options.explanation: Snowflake's default behavior prevents duplicate loading without FORCE = TRUE.

options.text: An error is raised indicating the file has been previously loaded.
options.explanation: Snowflake does not raise an error for previously loaded files — it silently skips them.

options.text: Snowflake deletes the file from the stage to prevent accidental reloads.
options.explanation: COPY INTO does not delete source files from stages.

options.text: Snowflake skips the file because it has already been loaded, as tracked in the load history metadata.
options.explanation: Snowflake tracks all files loaded via COPY INTO in the load history metadata (accessible through INFORMATION_SCHEMA.LOAD_HISTORY or COPY_HISTORY). By default, COPY INTO will skip any file that has already been loaded successfully, preventing duplicate data. The command completes without error but shows the file status as SKIPPED or LOADED.

questions.stem: A team wants to unload a Snowflake query result to an Amazon S3 bucket in Parquet format. Which command accomplishes this?

options.text: EXPORT TABLE my_table TO @my_s3_stage FILE_FORMAT = (TYPE = PARQUET);
options.explanation: EXPORT TABLE is not a Snowflake command.

options.text: COPY INTO @my_s3_stage FROM (SELECT * FROM my_table) FILE_FORMAT = (TYPE = PARQUET);
options.explanation: COPY INTO supports data unloading from a table or query to a stage (internal or external). The syntax `COPY INTO @stage FROM (SELECT ...)` unloads the query result to the specified stage location. Adding FILE_FORMAT = (TYPE = PARQUET) specifies the output format.

options.text: PUT @my_s3_stage SELECT * FROM my_table FILE_FORMAT = (TYPE = PARQUET);
options.explanation: PUT is for uploading local files to a stage, not for unloading query results.

options.text: UNLOAD SELECT * FROM my_table INTO @my_s3_stage FORMAT PARQUET;
options.explanation: UNLOAD is not a valid Snowflake SQL command.

questions.stem: A Snowpipe is configured with AUTO_INGEST = FALSE. How are files ingested with this configuration?

options.text: Files are never loaded; AUTO_INGEST = FALSE disables the pipe.
options.explanation: The pipe is fully functional with AUTO_INGEST = FALSE — it just requires an API call to trigger ingestion.

options.text: Files are ingested on a fixed schedule defined in the pipe definition.
options.explanation: Snowpipe does not have a built-in schedule; scheduling is done externally.

options.text: Files are ingested by calling the Snowpipe REST API endpoint insertFiles to submit file paths for loading.
options.explanation: When AUTO_INGEST = FALSE, Snowpipe does not listen for cloud event notifications. Instead, an external application or orchestration tool must call the Snowpipe REST API (specifically the insertFiles endpoint) with the list of file paths to be loaded. This is the REST API mode of Snowpipe.

options.text: Files are ingested automatically when they are uploaded to the internal stage associated with the pipe.
options.explanation: Snowpipe with AUTO_INGEST = FALSE does not monitor stage uploads — that behavior requires AUTO_INGEST = TRUE with cloud event notifications.

questions.stem: A data engineer is designing a data ingestion pipeline. Which TWO statements correctly distinguish COPY INTO from Snowpipe? (Select TWO)

options.text: COPY INTO requires an active virtual warehouse; Snowpipe uses Snowflake-managed serverless compute.
options.explanation: and **C** are correct. COPY INTO executes using a user-specified virtual warehouse and its associated credits, while Snowpipe uses Snowflake-managed serverless compute that is billed separately per file processed. Snowpipe is designed for continuous, low-latency ingestion of small batches of files as they arrive; COPY INTO is optimized for bulk loading large files in scheduled batch jobs.

options.text: COPY INTO tracks load history per file for 64 days; Snowpipe tracks load history for only 14 days.
options.explanation: Both COPY INTO and Snowpipe track load history, but the specific durations mentioned are not accurate — COPY INTO history is available in INFORMATION_SCHEMA for 14 days and in ACCOUNT_USAGE for 365 days.

options.text: Snowpipe is designed for continuous micro-batch loading; COPY INTO is best for bulk batch loads.
Snowpipe supports transformation logic via SELECT queries in the COPY statement; COPY INTO does not.
options.explanation: It is reversed — COPY INTO supports inline transformation SELECT queries, and Snowpipe also supports a limited subset of transformations in the pipe definition's COPY statement.

questions.stem: Which Snowflake role is the highest-privilege system-defined role and the only role that can manage account-level parameters and billing?

options.text: SYSADMIN
options.explanation: SYSADMIN manages database objects (warehouses, databases, schemas, tables) but does not have account-level billing access.

options.text: SECURITYADMIN
options.explanation: SECURITYADMIN manages users and roles but is a child of ACCOUNTADMIN in the hierarchy.

options.text: ACCOUNTADMIN
options.explanation: ACCOUNTADMIN is the top-level system-defined role in a Snowflake account. It encapsulates all other system roles and is the only role that can view and manage account-level parameters, billing information, resource monitors, and Snowflake's account usage data.

options.text: ORGADMIN
options.explanation: ORGADMIN is a special role for managing an organization (multiple accounts) and is not a standard account-level system role.

questions.stem: An administrator creates the following network policy: `CREATE NETWORK POLICY corp_policy ALLOWED_IP_LIST = ('192.168.1.0/24') BLOCKED_IP_LIST = ('192.168.1.99')`. A user connects from 192.168.1.99. What happens?

options.text: The connection is blocked because IPs in BLOCKED_IP_LIST are denied even if they fall within the ALLOWED_IP_LIST range.
options.explanation: In Snowflake network policies, BLOCKED_IP_LIST takes precedence over ALLOWED_IP_LIST. Even though 192.168.1.99 falls within the allowed subnet /24, the explicit block entry overrides the allow range, and the connection is denied.

options.text: The connection is allowed because the IP is within the allowed subnet 192.168.1.0/24.
options.explanation: The allowed range does not override the blocked list.

options.text: The connection is allowed because ALLOWED_IP_LIST takes precedence over BLOCKED_IP_LIST.
options.explanation: Snowflake's evaluation order is blocked first — blocked entries win.

options.text: An error is raised because a network policy cannot have overlapping allowed and blocked ranges.
options.explanation: Snowflake allows overlapping ranges in network policies; the blocked list simply takes precedence.

questions.stem: What is the purpose of SCIM (System for Cross-domain Identity Management) in Snowflake?

options.text: It enables single sign-on (SSO) authentication via SAML 2.0 for Snowflake logins.
options.explanation: SSO via SAML 2.0 is a different integration — SCIM handles user provisioning, while SAML handles authentication/login.

options.text: It allows automated provisioning and deprovisioning of Snowflake users and roles from an external identity provider like Okta or Azure AD.
options.explanation: SCIM (System for Cross-domain Identity Management) is a protocol that allows identity providers (like Okta, Azure AD, or OneLogin) to automatically create, update, and deactivate Snowflake users and groups based on changes in the IdP. This enables automated user lifecycle management.

options.text: It encrypts Snowflake credentials when stored in external secrets managers.
options.explanation: SCIM is not related to credential encryption or secrets management.

options.text: It provides federated identity so users can log into multiple cloud platforms with one Snowflake credential.
options.explanation: Federated identity across platforms is not what SCIM provides — it is specifically for user/role provisioning.

questions.stem: A company needs to ensure that a specific analyst can only see rows in the `sales` table where `region = 'APAC'`. Which Snowflake feature should be used?

options.text: Dynamic Data Masking
options.explanation: Dynamic Data Masking controls how column VALUES are displayed (e.g., masking SSNs) but does not filter out entire rows.

options.text: Column-level security
options.explanation: Column-level security restricts access to specific columns, not rows.

options.text: Row Access Policy
options.explanation: A Row Access Policy allows you to define a policy function that filters which rows a user (based on their role, attributes, or session context) can see in a table. When attached to the `sales` table, it can return only rows where region = 'APAC' for the analyst role.

options.text: Secure View
options.explanation: A secure view can filter rows, but using a Row Access Policy is the purpose-built, scalable approach that works across multiple tables/views without rewriting queries.

questions.stem: A developer is granted the role `data_engineer`. The role `data_engineer` has been granted to role `team_lead`. The role `team_lead` has been granted OWNERSHIP on database `analytics_db`. Which statement is TRUE about the developer's access to `analytics_db`?

options.text: The developer has OWNERSHIP on analytics_db only when their current session role is set to team_lead.
options.explanation: Inheritance means the developer gets the privilege through their `data_engineer` role — they do not need to switch to `team_lead`.

options.text: The developer has no access to analytics_db because OWNERSHIP cannot be inherited through role grants.
options.explanation: OWNERSHIP is inherited through the role hierarchy like other privileges.

options.text: The developer inherits the OWNERSHIP privilege on analytics_db through the role hierarchy when using the data_engineer role.
options.explanation: Snowflake's RBAC model uses role hierarchies where privileges flow upward. Since `data_engineer` is a child role of `team_lead`, the developer who has `data_engineer` inherits all privileges granted to `team_lead`, including OWNERSHIP on `analytics_db`. This inheritance applies when the session role is `data_engineer` (or any role that has `data_engineer` as a descendant).

options.text: The developer must be explicitly granted OWNERSHIP on analytics_db to access it.
options.explanation: The role hierarchy grants provide the access — an explicit direct grant is not required.

questions.stem: Which TWO actions can ONLY be performed by the ACCOUNTADMIN role in a Snowflake account? (Select TWO)

options.text: Creating a resource monitor and assigning it to a warehouse.
options.explanation: and **C** are correct. Resource monitors can only be created by ACCOUNTADMIN (or a role explicitly granted MANAGE GRANTS on resource monitors), and viewing ACCOUNT_USAGE data (which includes cross-account credit usage, login history, and query history) requires ACCOUNTADMIN or a role granted the SNOWFLAKE database usage privilege.

options.text: Creating a new database and granting USAGE on it to another role.
options.explanation: SYSADMIN can create databases and manage grants on those objects.

options.text: Viewing the ACCOUNT_USAGE schema to see historical credit consumption across the account.
Creating a new user with a temporary password.
options.explanation: SECURITYADMIN or USERADMIN can create users — this action is not exclusive to ACCOUNTADMIN.

questions.stem: Which Snowflake data type is used to store semi-structured data such as JSON, Avro, ORC, Parquet, and XML?

options.text: TEXT
options.explanation: TEXT (or VARCHAR) stores plain string data, not structured nested data with queryable paths.

options.text: VARIANT
options.explanation: VARIANT is Snowflake's universal data type for storing semi-structured data including JSON, Avro, Parquet, ORC, and XML. A VARIANT column can hold any of these formats and allows querying nested fields with dot notation and bracket notation.

options.text: STRUCT
options.explanation: STRUCT is not a native Snowflake data type — Snowflake uses VARIANT, OBJECT, and ARRAY instead.

options.text: BLOB
options.explanation: BLOB is not a native Snowflake type for semi-structured data.

questions.stem: A table column `event_data` is of type VARIANT and contains the following JSON: `{"user": {"id": 123, "name": "Alice"}, "action": "login"}`. Which query correctly extracts the user's name as a string?

options.text: SELECT event_data.user.name FROM events;
options.explanation: Dot notation works for traversing JSON paths in Snowflake, but the result is still a VARIANT type — however, `event_data:user:name::VARCHAR` (colon notation) is the standard Snowflake syntax, while `event_data.user.name` is not valid for VARIANT columns.

options.text: SELECT event_data['user']['name']::VARCHAR FROM events;
options.explanation: Snowflake uses bracket notation (event_data['user']['name']) to traverse nested JSON within a VARIANT column, and the ::VARCHAR cast converts the VARIANT result to a string.

options.text: SELECT JSON_VALUE(event_data, '$.user.name') FROM events;
options.explanation: JSON_VALUE() is not a native Snowflake SQL function — this is a SQL Server / ISO function.

options.text: SELECT EXTRACT(user.name FROM event_data) FROM events;
options.explanation: EXTRACT with this syntax is not valid Snowflake SQL for VARIANT data.

questions.stem: Which SQL function is used in Snowflake to convert a JSON string into a VARIANT value?

options.text: TO_VARIANT()
options.explanation: TO_VARIANT() converts other scalar types to a VARIANT representation, not a JSON string to a parsed VARIANT tree.

options.text: PARSE_JSON()
options.explanation: PARSE_JSON() is the Snowflake function that takes a JSON-formatted string and returns a VARIANT value containing the parsed JSON structure. This allows you to query nested fields using path syntax.

options.text: JSON_PARSE()
options.explanation: JSON_PARSE() is not a Snowflake function.

options.text: CAST_JSON()
options.explanation: CAST_JSON() is not a Snowflake function.

questions.stem: A VARIANT column contains an array of values: `[10, 20, 30, 40]`. Which Snowflake function is used to expand this array into individual rows?

options.text: SPLIT()
options.explanation: SPLIT() is a string function that splits a string on a delimiter; it does not flatten arrays stored in VARIANT columns.

options.text: UNNEST()
options.explanation: UNNEST() is a PostgreSQL/BigQuery function and is not a native Snowflake function.

options.text: FLATTEN()
options.explanation: FLATTEN() is the Snowflake table function that expands an ARRAY or OBJECT inside a VARIANT column into individual rows — one row per element. It is used with the LATERAL keyword in a FROM clause: `FROM table, LATERAL FLATTEN(INPUT => col)`.

options.text: EXPLODE()
options.explanation: EXPLODE() is a Spark SQL function and is not available in Snowflake.

questions.stem: A developer loads a JSON file into a VARIANT column and then tries to use the path expression `v:address:zip` to filter rows. Which TWO statements are TRUE about schema detection and querying in Snowflake semi-structured data? (Select TWO)

options.text: Snowflake can automatically detect and evolve the schema of JSON files using the INFER_SCHEMA function.
options.explanation: and **D** are correct. INFER_SCHEMA is a Snowflake table function that inspects staged files and infers their column names and data types, allowing schema-on-read or schema materialization. TRY_CAST() (and TRY_TO_*() functions) safely attempt type conversion and return NULL on failure instead of raising an error — this is important for VARIANT data that may have inconsistent types.

options.text: Path expressions on VARIANT columns always return a STRING type, requiring explicit casting for numeric comparisons.
options.explanation: VARIANT path expressions return VARIANT type, not STRING — the value retains its original type (NUMBER, BOOLEAN, etc.) inside the VARIANT wrapper; casting is needed to get a specific scalar type.

options.text: Snowflake's query optimizer can prune micro-partitions based on VARIANT column values if automatic schema detection is used and a schematized column is created.
options.explanation: is partially described but is misleading — standard micro-partition pruning works on native typed columns; VARIANT paths have limited pruning benefits unless the data is explicitly cast/materialized into typed columns or the Search Optimization Service is enabled.

options.text: TRY_CAST() can be used to safely convert VARIANT path values to a target type, returning NULL instead of an error if conversion fails.

questions.stem: A developer notices that a complex query is using the result cache every time it runs, but they need the query to always return fresh data from the table. How can they disable result cache usage for their session?

options.text: ALTER SESSION SET USE_CACHED_RESULT = FALSE;
options.explanation: The session parameter USE_CACHED_RESULT controls whether the query result cache is used for the current session. Setting it to FALSE forces queries to execute against the warehouse and not return a cached result, even if an identical query was recently run.

options.text: ALTER TABLE orders SET CACHE = DISABLED;
options.explanation: CACHE = DISABLED is not a valid table parameter in Snowflake.

options.text: Add the hint /*+ NO_RESULT_CACHE */ to the SELECT statement.
options.explanation: Snowflake does not support query hints like /*+ NO_RESULT_CACHE */ in the SQL standard hint format.

options.text: SET RESULT_CACHE_ACTIVE = 0;
options.explanation: RESULT_CACHE_ACTIVE is not a valid Snowflake parameter name.

questions.stem: A query in the Query Profile shows the 'most expensive node' is a table scan that scanned 100% of micro-partitions (0 pruned). The WHERE clause filters on a high-cardinality column `customer_id` using an equality predicate. What is the BEST single action to improve micro-partition pruning for this use case?

options.text: Increase the virtual warehouse size to scan more micro-partitions faster.
options.explanation: Increasing warehouse size does not reduce the number of micro-partitions scanned — it makes the full scan faster but does not add pruning intelligence.

options.text: Create a clustering key on `customer_id` to improve micro-partition pruning.
options.explanation: Clustering keys help most with range scans on ordered columns (such as dates). For random equality lookups on a high-cardinality column like `customer_id`, Search Optimization Service is more effective.

options.text: Enable the Search Optimization Service on the `customer_id` column for point-lookup queries.
options.explanation: The Search Optimization Service is purpose-built for improving equality predicate (point-lookup) performance on high-cardinality columns like `customer_id`. It builds a persistent search access path that allows Snowflake to skip the vast majority of micro-partitions when filtering on a specific customer ID value.

options.text: Convert the table to a transient table to reduce storage overhead during scans.
options.explanation: Converting to a transient table changes data protection characteristics but has no effect on query performance or micro-partition pruning.

questions.stem: A materialized view is defined over a table that receives frequent DML (inserts, updates, deletes). Which statement BEST describes how Snowflake maintains the materialized view?

options.text: Snowflake refreshes the materialized view synchronously with every DML operation on the base table, consuming warehouse credits.
options.explanation: The refresh is not synchronous with each DML — it is asynchronous background maintenance.

options.text: Snowflake automatically maintains the materialized view in the background using serverless compute, keeping it in sync with the base table without user intervention.
options.explanation: Snowflake automatically maintains materialized views using background serverless compute (similar to automatic clustering). When the base table changes via DML, Snowflake detects the changes and incrementally updates the materialized view without requiring any user action. This background maintenance is billed as serverless credit usage.

options.text: The user must manually call ALTER MATERIALIZED VIEW ... REFRESH to keep the view current after each DML operation.
options.explanation: Users do not need to manually refresh Snowflake materialized views — automatic maintenance is provided.

options.text: Materialized views are not recommended for tables with frequent DML because Snowflake does not support automatic refresh.
options.explanation: Snowflake does support automatic refresh of materialized views for DML-active tables.

questions.stem: A data engineer reviews a query profile and sees a 'spillage to local storage' warning and an 'spillage to remote storage' warning. Which TWO actions would MOST likely reduce spillage? (Select TWO)

options.text: Increase the virtual warehouse size to provide more in-memory space for query operations.
options.explanation: and **B** are correct. Spillage occurs when intermediate query results exceed available in-memory space on the virtual warehouse nodes and must be written to local SSD (local spillage) or remote object storage (remote spillage). Increasing the warehouse size adds more memory and CPU per node, reducing the likelihood of spillage for memory-intensive operations like large joins and aggregations. Rewriting the query to apply more selective filters earlier reduces the volume of data that needs to be held in memory at once.

options.text: Rewrite the query to reduce the result set size early with more selective WHERE filters.
Enable the Query Acceleration Service to offload the full query to serverless compute.
options.explanation: The Query Acceleration Service can help certain queries with large table scans but does not directly address in-memory spillage from join/aggregation operations.

options.text: Decrease the AUTO_SUSPEND timeout to free up memory between queries.
options.explanation: AUTO_SUSPEND affects idle warehouses only and has no impact on memory available during active query execution.

questions.stem: What is the primary mechanism Snowflake uses to share live data between accounts without copying data?

options.text: Secure Data Sharing
options.explanation: Secure Data Sharing allows a data provider account to share live, read-only access to database objects (tables, views, UDFs) with consumer accounts without copying or moving data. Consumers query the provider's data directly through Snowflake's shared metadata layer.

options.text: Replication
options.explanation: Replication duplicates data across regions or clouds to separate accounts — it is used for disaster recovery and business continuity, not for collaborative real-time data sharing within a region.

options.text: Zero-Copy Cloning
options.explanation: Zero-Copy Cloning creates an independent copy (within the same account), not a cross-account share.

options.text: External Tables
options.explanation: External tables point to data in external storage and are not the mechanism for cross-account sharing.

questions.stem: In Snowflake data sharing, what is a 'reader account'?

options.text: A Snowflake internal role that has read-only access to all tables.
options.explanation: A reader account is an account-level entity, not a role.

options.text: A managed Snowflake account created by a provider for consumers who do not have a Snowflake account of their own.
options.explanation: A reader account is a managed Snowflake account that a data provider creates to enable consumers without their own Snowflake account to access shared data. The provider administers the reader account and is billed for compute used by reader account queries.

options.text: An external database connection that reads data from Snowflake via JDBC.
options.explanation: Reader accounts are full Snowflake accounts (albeit managed and limited), not external JDBC connections.

options.text: A special type of Snowflake account that can only receive but not publish data shares.
options.explanation: The specific purpose of a reader account is to serve non-Snowflake customers — the name 'reader account' does not describe a general consumer account type.

questions.stem: A data provider wants to share data from a table that contains PII. They want consumers to see aggregated, anonymized data only. Which object should the provider add to the share?

options.text: The underlying base table directly.
options.explanation: Sharing the base table would expose raw PII data to consumers.

options.text: A secure view that filters and aggregates the data to remove PII.
options.explanation: Secure views are the recommended approach for sharing transformed or filtered data. A secure view prevents consumers from seeing the view's underlying SQL definition (protecting the provider's logic) while allowing them to query the view's output. The provider can embed aggregation and PII-removal logic in the secure view.

options.text: A transient table containing pre-computed anonymized data.
options.explanation: A transient table would require periodic data refresh pipelines, introduces data duplication, and may not be current.

options.text: A dynamic table that refreshes anonymized data every 5 minutes.
options.explanation: While a dynamic table could work technically, the standard and recommended pattern for this scenario is a secure view in a share.

questions.stem: When a Snowflake data consumer accesses a share that was published by a provider, what is the impact on the provider's virtual warehouses?

options.text: The provider's virtual warehouse executes the consumer's queries, consuming the provider's credits.
options.explanation: Queries run on the consumer's compute, not the provider's.

options.text: Snowflake provisions a shared serverless compute pool paid jointly by provider and consumer.
options.explanation: There is no shared compute pool — each party uses their own resources.

options.text: The consumer's own virtual warehouse executes the queries against the shared data, consuming the consumer's credits.
options.explanation: In Snowflake Secure Data Sharing, the consumer's virtual warehouse executes all queries against the shared data objects. The provider's warehouses are not involved in query execution. The consumer pays for their own compute credits. This is a key advantage of Snowflake's sharing model — the provider incurs no compute cost for consumer queries.

options.text: Data sharing requires no compute — queries run entirely from cache in the Cloud Services layer.
options.explanation: Queries do require compute (the consumer's warehouse) — the data is not purely served from cache.

questions.stem: A Snowflake provider creates a share and adds an account `XYZ123` as a consumer. What must the consumer's ACCOUNTADMIN do to access the shared data?

options.text: Run IMPORT SHARE to accept the share, then create a database from the share.
options.explanation: The consumer's ACCOUNTADMIN must first run `SHOW SHARES` to see available shares, then `CREATE DATABASE my_db FROM SHARE provider_account.share_name` to create a local database object that maps to the share. This makes the shared data accessible in the consumer's account. Some workflows also use `IMPORT SHARE` syntax depending on the interface.

options.text: Run CLONE DATABASE to copy the shared data locally before querying it.
options.explanation: Cloning creates a copy — in data sharing, the data is never copied to the consumer's account.

options.text: Submit a data access request through the Snowflake Marketplace and wait for approval.
options.explanation: Direct account-to-account shares do not go through the Marketplace listing process; that applies to Marketplace listings, not direct shares.

options.text: Connect to the provider's account via a database link to query the tables.
options.explanation: Snowflake does not use database links; the share is accessed by creating a database from the share.

questions.stem: What is the Snowflake Marketplace?

options.text: An internal Snowflake feature store where data engineers publish reusable ETL pipelines.
options.explanation: The Marketplace is not an internal ETL feature store.

options.text: A platform where data providers can publish data products (listings) for other Snowflake customers to discover and access.
options.explanation: The Snowflake Marketplace is a data sharing ecosystem where providers (companies, organizations, or individuals) can publish data listings that Snowflake customers can discover, request, and access. Data is shared live without copying using Snowflake's Secure Data Sharing technology.

options.text: A cloud storage marketplace integrated with S3, Azure Blob, and GCS for purchasing data sets.
options.explanation: The Marketplace is not a storage procurement platform — it is specifically for sharing Snowflake data.

options.text: A Snowflake-managed database where Snowflake publishes reference datasets for all customers.
options.explanation: The Marketplace hosts third-party provider listings; Snowflake itself does publish some datasets, but the Marketplace is primarily a third-party ecosystem.

questions.stem: A company wants to share a database with multiple Snowflake consumer accounts using Data Sharing. Which TWO statements about objects that can be included in a share are CORRECT? (Select TWO)

options.text: External tables (referencing data in S3) can be included in a share.
options.explanation: and **C** are correct. External tables can be included in a share, allowing consumers to query data that physically resides in the provider's cloud storage. Only SECURE views (declared with CREATE SECURE VIEW) can be shared; regular views are blocked from shares because they would expose the underlying SQL definition to consumers — secure views prevent this by hiding the view definition.

options.text: Transient tables can be added to a share and consumers can query them.
options.explanation: is wrong not because transient tables cannot be shared (they technically can), but rather the more important distinction is about SECURE views.

options.text: Only SECURE views (not regular views) can be added to a share that exposes view logic.
A share can only contain objects from a single database in the provider's account.
options.explanation: A share can include objects from multiple databases in the same account by using cross-database object references in secure views.

questions.stem: A provider wants to charge consumers for accessing a Snowflake Marketplace listing. Which monetization option does Snowflake Marketplace support?

options.text: Providers can charge consumers directly by setting a credit-per-query rate inside Snowflake.
options.explanation: There is no per-query credit rate mechanism set inside the provider's account.

options.text: Providers can create paid listings where consumers are billed through the Snowflake Marketplace's integrated payment and billing system.
options.explanation: The Snowflake Marketplace supports paid listings where Snowflake handles the billing between provider and consumer through its integrated payment system. Providers can set pricing (subscription, usage-based, etc.) and Snowflake manages the transaction.

options.text: Providers must establish billing agreements outside of Snowflake; the Marketplace only supports free listings.
options.explanation: The Marketplace does support monetized (paid) listings natively — not just free listings.

options.text: Providers can sell access tokens that consumers redeem to unlock a share for a limited duration.
options.explanation: Access tokens are not the monetization mechanism used by the Snowflake Marketplace.

questions.stem: A Snowflake data provider publishes a listing on the Snowflake Marketplace. A consumer account is in the same cloud region. A consumer account from a different cloud provider and region also wants to access the same listing. Which statement is TRUE?

options.text: Data sharing is restricted to accounts within the same cloud provider and region; cross-region sharing is not supported.
options.explanation: Snowflake does support cross-region and cross-cloud data sharing through its global infrastructure.

options.text: Cross-region and cross-cloud sharing is supported through Snowflake's replication infrastructure, which the Marketplace handles transparently.
options.explanation: Snowflake Marketplace supports cross-region and cross-cloud data sharing.
